system_examination_technique.approach: Patient supine, fully exposed limbs. Proceed systematically: General Inspection → Higher Mental Functions → Cranial Nerves → Motor (Inspection → Tone → Power → Bulk) → Sensory → Reflexes & Plantar → Coordination & Cerebellar → Gait. Always compare both sides. Determine UMN vs LMN first.
system_examination_technique.sequence: General Inspection → Higher Mental Functions → Cranial Nerves → Motor (Inspection → Tone → Power) → Sensory → Reflexes & Plantar → Coordination → Gait

actions: From foot of bed: consciousness, orientation, posture (Wernicke-Mann posture — arm flexed, leg extended = UMN; frog-leg posture = LMN), wasting (symmetric vs asymmetric), fasciculations (watch 30 seconds), involuntary movements (tremor, chorea, dystonia).
Devices: catheter (neurogenic bladder), wheelchair, orthoses (AFO, KAFO), nasogastric tube (bulbar weakness), tracheostomy.
Bedside clues: walking aids, pressure sores (sacrum, heels = immobility), contractures, muscle wasting pattern.
Spine: gibbus (Pott's), scoliosis, scars, sinuses. Palpate spinous processes for tenderness.
Level of consciousness: GCS or alert/drowsy/stuporous/comatose.
Orientation: time, place, person.

Speech: Broca's aphasia (non-fluent, comprehension preserved, repetition impaired — frontal lobe), Wernicke's aphasia (fluent but nonsensical, comprehension impaired — temporal lobe), global aphasia (both affected — large MCA territory).
Neglect: double simultaneous stimulation (parietal lobe, non-dominant hemisphere).
CN I (Olfactory): Test each nostril with coffee/cloves. Anosmia = frontal lobe lesion, post-trauma.

CN II (Optic): Visual acuity (Snellen), visual fields (confrontation), fundoscopy (papilledema, optic atrophy, disc pallor), pupillary reflexes (direct, consensual, RAPD/Marcus Gunn pupil with swinging flashlight test).

CN III, IV, VI (Eye movements): Follow H-pattern. CN III palsy = ptosis + dilated pupil + eye down-and-out. CN IV = diplopia on looking down and in (reading/stairs). CN VI = medial deviation, cannot abduct. Nystagmus: direction, type.
CN V (Trigeminal): Sensory — light touch and pinprick in V1/V2/V3. Motor — masseter/temporalis (clench jaw). Corneal reflex (afferent V1, efferent VII). Jaw jerk (brisk = bilateral UMN).
CN VII (Facial): Upper vs lower face distinction. 'Raise eyebrows' (UMN spares forehead, LMN affects all). 'Show teeth', 'puff cheeks', 'close eyes tight'.
CN VIII (Vestibulocochlear): Rinne and Weber tests. Sensorineural vs conductive hearing loss.
CN IX, X (Glossopharyngeal, Vagus): 'Say ah' — uvula deviates AWAY from lesion. Gag reflex. Palatal movement. Nasal speech = palatal weakness.
CN XI (Accessory): Shrug shoulders against resistance (trapezius). Turn head against resistance (sternocleidomastoid — turns head to OPPOSITE side).
CN XII (Hypoglossal): 'Stick out tongue'. LMN: tongue deviates TOWARDS lesion (wasting + fasciculations on affected side). UMN: tongue deviates AWAY from lesion.
Posture: Wernicke-Mann (UMN — arm flexed at elbow/wrist, leg extended), frog-leg (LMN — bilateral hip abduction/external rotation from hypotonia).

Wasting: Compare both sides. Measure limb circumference at fixed points. Generalized (disuse) vs focal (denervation). Thenar/hypothenar (T1, median/ulnar), dorsal interossei (ulnar), quadriceps (femoral).
Fasciculations: Rippling contractions under skin = anterior horn cell disease (MND) or nerve root compression. Watch each muscle group for 30 seconds in good light.
Patient must be completely relaxed. Support limb and perform passive movements at each joint.

Spasticity (UMN): Velocity-dependent, clasp-knife quality (resistance increases then suddenly gives way). Test by rapid passive flexion/extension at elbow and knee. Predominant in flexors of upper limb, extensors of lower limb.
Rigidity (extrapyramidal): Velocity-independent, lead-pipe (uniform resistance throughout range) or cogwheel (lead-pipe + superimposed tremor). Present in ALL directions of movement.
Hypotonia (LMN/cerebellar): Floppy limb, no resistance to passive movement.
Clonus: Rapidly dorsiflex ankle and maintain pressure. Sustained clonus (≥3 beats) = pathological UMN sign. Unsustained (1-2 beats) may be normal in anxious patients.
MRC grading: 0 = no contraction, 1 = flicker/trace, 2 = movement with gravity eliminated, 3 = against gravity, 4 = against resistance (4-, 4, 4+), 5 = normal.
Pyramidal pattern (UMN): Upper limb extensors weaker than flexors (wrist/finger drop), lower limb flexors weaker than extensors (hip flexion, knee flexion, ankle dorsiflexion weak).

Test systematically: Shoulder abduction (C5), elbow flexion (C5-C6), elbow extension (C7), wrist extension (C6-C7), grip (C8-T1), finger abduction (T1). Hip flexion (L1-L2), knee extension (L3-L4), ankle dorsiflexion (L4-L5), ankle plantarflexion (S1-S2), great toe extension (L5).
Pronator drift test: Arms extended, palms up, eyes closed 10 seconds. Affected arm pronates and drifts downward = subtle pyramidal weakness (detects grade 4+ weakness that formal testing may miss).
Spinothalamic tract (lateral): Pain (pinprick — NEW pin each patient) and temperature (cold tuning fork). Crosses 1-2 segments above entry → contralateral loss.

Dorsal columns (posterior): Vibration (128Hz tuning fork on bony prominences — medial malleolus → tibial tuberosity → ASIS → sternum) and joint position sense (start with great toe DIP). Ipsilateral loss.
Light touch: Carried by BOTH anterior spinothalamic and dorsal columns — lost only when both pathways damaged.
Cortical sensory functions: Two-point discrimination, stereognosis (identify object in hand), graphesthesia (identify number drawn on palm). Requires intact primary sensation.
Finding the sensory level: Start from insensate area and move UPWARD until patient reports change. Repeat from normal area DOWNWARD. Two points should converge at same dermatome.
Key dermatome landmarks: C4 = cape/shoulders, T4 = nipple, T6 = xiphisternum, T10 = umbilicus, L1 = inguinal ligament, L3 = medial knee, L5 = dorsum of foot, S1 = lateral foot, S3-S5 = perianal.
Dissociated sensory loss: Pain/temperature lost with preserved proprioception → anterior cord/syringomyelia. Proprioception lost with preserved pain → posterior cord (B12 deficiency, tabes dorsalis).
Deep tendon reflexes — root values: Biceps (C5-C6), Triceps (C6-C7-C8), Supinator/Brachioradialis (C5-C6), Knee (L2-L3-L4), Ankle (L5-S1-S2).
Grading: 0 = absent, 1+ = diminished (with reinforcement), 2+ = normal, 3+ = brisk without clonus, 4+ = clonus. Always compare sides.
Reinforcement (Jendrassik maneuver): If absent reflexes — ask patient to clench teeth or interlock fingers and pull apart. Reduces descending inhibition on spinal reflexes.
Inverted reflex: Absent reflex at one level + exaggerated reflex below = lesion AT that segment (LMN at level, UMN below). Example: absent biceps jerk with brisk triceps = C5-C6 lesion.
Hoffmann's sign (upper limb Babinski equivalent): Flick middle finger DIP downward — positive = involuntary thumb/index flexion. Asymmetric positive = definite UMN.
Superficial reflexes: Abdominal (T7-T12) — absent below cord level. Cremasteric (L1-L2). Anal reflex (S4-S5).

Plantar response (Babinski): Stroke lateral border of sole from heel → across metatarsal heads. Extensor (big toe dorsiflexion + toe fanning) = UMN. Flexor = normal. Mechanism: UMN lesion disinhibits the primitive flexion-withdrawal reflex.
Finger-nose test: Intention tremor (worsens approaching target) = cerebellar hemisphere lesion. Past-pointing = ipsilateral cerebellar hemisphere.
Heel-shin test: Run heel smoothly down opposite shin. Incoordination = ipsilateral cerebellar hemisphere.
Rapid alternating movements (dysdiadochokinesia): Tap palm alternately with palm/dorsum of other hand. Irregular rhythm/amplitude = cerebellar.
Rebound phenomenon: Patient pushes against your hand → suddenly release → affected arm overshoots (loss of check) = cerebellar.

Romberg's test: Stand feet together, eyes open then closed. Positive Romberg (falls with eyes closed) = SENSORY ataxia (proprioceptive loss — posterior columns). Cerebellar ataxia → unsteady with eyes OPEN AND closed.
Vermis vs hemisphere: Vermis = truncal ataxia, broad-based gait, no limb ataxia. Hemisphere = ipsilateral limb ataxia (dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia).
Hemiplegic gait (circumduction): Spastic leg swings outward in semicircle, foot scrapes floor. Arm held flexed. = UMN hemisphere lesion.
Spastic paraparetic gait (scissoring): Both legs stiff and adducted, crossing midline. = bilateral cord lesion.
High-stepping (steppage) gait: Foot drop → lifts knee high to clear foot. = Common peroneal nerve palsy or L5 radiculopathy.
Waddling gait: Trunk sways side to side. = Proximal myopathy (hip girdle weakness — Trendelenburg).
Ataxic gait (cerebellar): Broad-based, unsteady, irregular stride, cannot tandem walk. = Cerebellar vermis.
Sensory ataxic gait: High-stepping, stamping, worse in dark/eyes closed. = Posterior column disease (B12 deficiency, tabes dorsalis).
Parkinsonian gait: Short shuffling steps, reduced arm swing, difficulty initiating (freezing), festination (progressively shorter faster steps).
Tandem gait (heel-to-toe): Most sensitive test for mild cerebellar dysfunction.

values: Pyramidal distribution (extensors weak in arm, flexors weak in leg)
Absent or late (disuse atrophy after months)
Face + Arm >> Leg (or Leg >> Arm if ACA)
Absent: pure motor deficit without cortical sensory signs
May have hemianopia if posterior limb involved
Variable — arm often recovers less than leg
Often poor (dense motor pathway damage in compact area)
Knee jerk preserved, ankle jerk lost
Both may be lost depending on root level

presentation_script.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 58-year-old male who is conscious and oriented with Broca's aphasia.
presentation_script.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is conscious, oriented, with non-fluent aphasia. Pulse is irregularly irregular at 88/min suggesting atrial fibrillation. No pallor, icterus, cyanosis, clubbing, or pedal edema.
presentation_script.system_findings: On neurological examination, there is right-sided UMN facial palsy with lower face weakness and forehead sparing. Motor examination reveals spastic tone in the right upper and lower limbs with clasp-knife spasticity. Power is 2/5 in the right upper limb and 3/5 in the right lower limb, with face and arm weaker than leg suggesting MCA territory. Deep tendon reflexes are exaggerated on the right side with sustained ankle clonus. Plantar response is extensor on the right (Babinski positive) and flexor on the left. There is cortical sensory loss on the right side with impaired two-point discrimination and astereognosis. Gait is circumduction type.
presentation_script.diagnosis_statement: This patient has right-sided upper motor neuron type hemiplegia with UMN facial palsy, most likely due to a left middle cerebral artery territory ischemic stroke, as evidenced by right-sided spastic hemiparesis (face, arm > leg), UMN 7th nerve palsy, brisk deep tendon reflexes, extensor plantar response, circumduction gait, and Broca's aphasia, in the setting of hypertension and atrial fibrillation.
differentials: Left MCA territory ischemic stroke — cardioembolic (AF)
Left MCA territory ischemic stroke — large artery atherosclerosis
Left internal capsule lacunar infarct (but would be pure motor, equal face/arm/leg, no cortical signs)
Left MCA territory hemorrhagic stroke (but no headache/vomiting/LOC at onset)
Space-occupying lesion (tumor, abscess) — but sudden onset favors vascular
tips: Always state Side + UMN/LMN type + Topographic diagnosis (cortical/capsular/brainstem) + Etiological diagnosis (infarct/hemorrhage) + Supporting signs + Risk factors.
Face + arm > leg = MCA territory. Leg > arm = ACA territory. Equal face/arm/leg = internal capsule.
In acute stroke (<24h), reflexes may be normal or depressed (spinal shock/diaschisis) — do not exclude stroke based on normal reflexes acutely.

case_scenario.chief_complaint: Right-sided weakness of body and difficulty in speaking for 3 months
case_scenario.hpi: Patient woke up 3 months ago with sudden onset right-sided weakness involving face, arm, and leg, with arm weaker than leg. Associated difficulty in speaking (non-fluent speech, comprehension relatively preserved). No headache, vomiting, or loss of consciousness at onset. Known hypertensive for 12 years and diabetic for 8 years. Known to have irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) — not on anticoagulation.
case_scenario.past_history: Hypertension 12 years (on amlodipine), Type 2 DM 8 years (on metformin), Atrial fibrillation — not anticoagulated
case_scenario.drug_history: Amlodipine 5mg, Metformin 500mg BD, Aspirin 75mg (started after stroke)

clinical_summary.detail: Non-fluent speech, comprehension preserved, repetition impaired, irregularly irregular pulse (AF)
clinical_summary.significance: Left hemisphere cortical lesion + cardiac source of embolism

clinical_summary.detail: Lower face weak, forehead spared. No other CN deficit
clinical_summary.significance: UMN 7th = supranuclear lesion. Forehead spared because upper face has bilateral cortical representation

clinical_summary.detail: Clasp-knife type in right UL and LL. Flexor pattern in arm, extensor pattern in leg (Wernicke-Mann posture)
clinical_summary.significance: UMN lesion → spasticity. Wernicke-Mann: arm flexed, leg extended

clinical_summary.detail: Right UL 2/5 (arm > forearm), Right LL 3/5 (hip > knee > ankle). Left side 5/5
clinical_summary.significance: Face and arm > leg = MCA territory. Leg > arm = ACA territory

clinical_summary.detail: Exaggerated biceps, triceps, supinator, knee, ankle jerks on right. Left normal
clinical_summary.significance: UMN type hyperreflexia — loss of descending inhibition on spinal reflexes

clinical_summary.detail: Right Babinski positive. Left flexor
clinical_summary.significance: The hallmark UMN sign — disinhibition of the flexion withdrawal reflex

clinical_summary.detail: Right leg swings outward in semicircle, foot scrapes floor. Right arm held in flexion
clinical_summary.significance: Spastic leg cannot flex adequately at hip/knee → compensatory circumduction

clinical_summary.detail: Right side: impaired 2-point discrimination, astereognosis, agraphesthesia
clinical_summary.significance: Parietal cortex involvement (MCA territory includes somatosensory cortex)

examination_technique.sequence: General Inspection → Cranial Nerves (especially VII) → Motor (Tone → Power → Pronator drift) → Reflexes & Plantar → Sensory → Coordination → Gait

actions: Observe Wernicke-Mann posture: shoulder adducted, elbow flexed, wrist flexed, fingers curled (UL); hip extended, knee extended, ankle plantarflexed (LL).
Note wasting: In chronic hemiplegia, disuse atrophy may be visible. Measure limb circumference at fixed points and compare sides.
Fasciculations: Absent in UMN lesion. If present → consider LMN component or MND.
findings_in_this_case: Mild disuse atrophy of right limbs (3 months duration)

actions: Ensure patient is relaxed. Support forearm and passively flex/extend elbow slowly then rapidly. Note velocity-dependent increase (spasticity) vs uniform resistance (rigidity).
Test ankle clonus: rapidly dorsiflex foot and maintain pressure. Sustained ≥3 beats = pathological UMN sign.
In acute stroke (<24-48h), tone may be flaccid (spinal shock/diaschisis) before spasticity emerges.
findings_in_this_case: Right-sided clasp-knife spasticity at elbow and knee
Sustained ankle clonus on right (>5 beats)

actions: Test MRC grading in pyramidal distribution: UL extensors (wrist/finger extension) and LL flexors (hip flexion, knee flexion, ankle dorsiflexion) are preferentially weak in UMN.
Pronator drift test: arms extended, palms up, eyes closed 10 seconds. Affected arm pronates and drifts down — detects subtle grade 4+ weakness.
Pseudoweakness (pain, non-cooperation) causes variable inconsistent power; true pyramidal weakness is consistent and follows flexor-extensor pattern.
findings_in_this_case: Right UL: 2/5 (arm > forearm), right LL: 3/5 (hip > knee > ankle)

actions: Test all DTRs bilaterally. Grade 0-4+. Compare sides — asymmetry is as important as absolute value.
Hoffmann's sign: flick middle finger DIP downward — positive = involuntary thumb/index flexion. Asymmetric positive = definite UMN.
Babinski: stroke lateral sole from heel → across metatarsal heads. Extensor = UMN. In acute stroke (<24h), reflexes may be normal or depressed.
Ask patient to walk if safe. Observe circumduction pattern: spastic leg swings outward in semicircle because it cannot flex at hip/knee.
Note reduced arm swing on affected side.
Distinguish from foot drop (isolated dorsiflexor weakness = peroneal nerve palsy, NOT stroke — stroke produces complete pyramidal pattern weakness).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: UMN lesion removes descending inhibition from reticulospinal tract on spinal stretch reflex → hyperexcitable alpha motor neurons → velocity-dependent increase in muscle tone
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Cortical/capsular lesion → loss of descending inhibition → hyperactive stretch reflex → spasticity
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: In acute stroke (<48h), tone may be flaccid (diaschisis). Spasticity emerges over 24-72 hours as spinal circuits recover from shock.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Corticospinal tract normally INHIBITS the flexion-withdrawal reflex. UMN lesion releases this primitive reflex → big toe dorsiflexes as part of triple flexion response (hip, knee, ankle all flex)
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Corticospinal tract damage → loss of inhibition → primitive flexion-withdrawal reflex re-emerges → big toe extends + toes fan
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Withdrawal response (all toes flex with hip/knee flexion) is NOT a true Babinski. In acute stroke, may take hours to emerge.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Upper facial nucleus receives bilateral cortical input; lower facial nucleus receives only contralateral input. Cortical/capsular lesion → only contralateral lower face weakens, forehead spared
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Left hemisphere lesion → right lower facial nucleus loses contralateral input → lower face weak. Upper facial nucleus still receives ipsilateral input → forehead preserved
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: In LMN (Bell's palsy), BOTH upper and lower face affected ipsilaterally because the nerve itself is damaged below the nucleus.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Broca's area (posterior inferior frontal gyrus, Brodmann area 44-45) in dominant (left) hemisphere is the motor speech area. Lesion → non-fluent speech with preserved comprehension
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Left MCA territory stroke → Broca's area damaged → loss of motor programming for speech → non-fluent, telegraphic speech with intact comprehension
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Right hemisphere lesions in left-handed patients can cause aphasia (crossed aphasia). Right hemisphere lesions cause neglect, not aphasia, in right-handed patients.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Spastic leg held in extension cannot flex at hip/knee. To clear the foot, patient tilts pelvis and swings the stiff leg outward in semicircular arc
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: UMN lesion → spastic extension of leg → cannot flex hip/knee/ankle during swing phase → compensatory circumduction + foot scraping
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Isolated foot drop (peroneal palsy) causes steppage gait, NOT circumduction — because hip/knee are normal.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Loss of descending corticospinal inhibition on spinal reflex arcs → exaggerated monosynaptic stretch reflex
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: UMN lesion → loss of descending inhibition → spinal reflex arc hyperexcitable → brisk reflexes
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: In acute stroke (<24h), reflexes may be normal or depressed due to spinal shock/diaschisis. Hyperreflexia emerges over 24-72 hours.

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: Left middle cerebral artery territory ischemic stroke (cardioembolic — atrial fibrillation) with right UMN hemiplegia, UMN facial palsy, and Broca's aphasia, now in the established stroke (chronic) phase
supporting_evidence: Right-sided UMN signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski, circumduction gait)
Face + arm > leg distribution = MCA territory
Broca's aphasia = left hemisphere (dominant) involvement
Atrial fibrillation = cardioembolic source
Sudden onset during sleep = vascular etiology
No headache/vomiting/LOC = against hemorrhagic (though not absolute)

differentials.why_not: Face + arm > leg (not equal), cortical signs present (aphasia, cortical sensory loss) — capsular lesions produce pure motor hemiplegia WITHOUT cortical signs
No headache, vomiting, or LOC at onset (hemorrhagic strokes usually have these). CT brain would differentiate.
Sudden onset favors vascular. SOL typically has progressive course with features of raised ICP (headache, vomiting, papilledema).
No ipsilateral cranial nerve palsy (crossed signs). No cerebellar signs. UMN facial palsy (not LMN). Aphasia = cortical, not brainstem.

investigations.expected: Hypodense area in left MCA territory (established infarct). Hemorrhage would be hyperdense (white)
investigations.why: First investigation in acute stroke to EXCLUDE hemorrhage

investigations.expected: Restricted diffusion in left MCA territory. Gold standard for acute ischemic stroke — detects within minutes
investigations.why: Confirms ischemic infarct location and extent

investigations.expected: May show MCA occlusion or stenosis. Carotid stenosis assessment
investigations.why: Identifies occluded vessel. Essential for thrombectomy planning

investigations.expected: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no P waves, fibrillation baseline)
investigations.why: Confirms cardioembolic source. 24h Holter if paroxysmal AF suspected

investigations.expected: LA enlargement, LA appendage thrombus (TEE superior). Rule out valvular disease, LV thrombus, PFO
investigations.why: Identifies cardiac source of embolism

investigations.expected: Carotid stenosis quantification. >70% stenosis → consider carotid endarterectomy
investigations.why: Assess for large artery atherosclerosis

investigations.expected: CBC, RBS, HbA1c, lipid profile, renal function, coagulation profile
investigations.why: Baseline assessment and risk factor quantification

medical.rationale: Cardioembolic stroke from AF — anticoagulation prevents recurrence
medical.specifics: Start warfarin/DOAC after 2-14 days (delay for large infarct — hemorrhagic transformation risk). DOACs preferred for non-valvular AF. Target INR 2-3 for warfarin.
BP target <130/80 (SPRINT), HbA1c <7%, LDL <70 (high-dose statin), smoking cessation
Physiotherapy (strengthen weak limbs, prevent contractures), occupational therapy (ADL retraining), speech therapy (Broca's aphasia), psychological support (post-stroke depression in 30%)

management.interventional: Thrombolysis (IV alteplase 0.9mg/kg within 4.5h) and mechanical thrombectomy (LVO, up to 24h with perfusion imaging) — in the acute phase
management.surgical: Decompressive craniectomy for malignant MCA infarction with cerebral edema. Carotid endarterectomy if symptomatic carotid stenosis >70%.
management.this_patient: This patient is 3 months post-stroke — now in the chronic/rehabilitation phase. Anticoagulation for AF (DOAC preferred), risk factor optimization, ongoing rehabilitation. Dual antiplatelet not needed since AF is the etiology (anticoagulation replaces antiplatelet).
management.follow_up: Regular INR monitoring if on warfarin. CHA2DS2-VASc score assessment. HbA1c and BP monitoring. Rehabilitation progress assessment. Screen for post-stroke depression.

complications.mechanism: Reperfusion of infarcted tissue → damaged blood vessels leak → bleeding into infarct
complications.management: Repeat CT. Reverse anticoagulation if on warfarin. Supportive care.

complications.mechanism: Biological (neurotransmitter disruption) + psychological (disability, loss of independence)
complications.presentation: Affects 30% of stroke survivors. Apathy, anhedonia, sleep disturbance

complications.mechanism: Chronic UMN lesion → sustained spasticity → muscle fibrosis → fixed contracture
complications.presentation: Fixed joint deformity, pain, hygiene problems in hand
complications.management: Physiotherapy, botulinum toxin, baclofen, serial casting

complications.presentation: Leg swelling, Homan's sign, dyspnea/chest pain (PE)
complications.management: LMWH prophylaxis, compression stockings, early mobilization

vivas.question: How do you differentiate UMN from LMN facial palsy?
vivas.answer: The facial nerve nucleus has two parts: upper half (frontalis, orbicularis oculi) receives BILATERAL cortical input; lower half (lower face) receives only CONTRALATERAL input. In UMN lesion: contralateral lower face weak, forehead spared. In LMN lesion (Bell's palsy, CPA tumor): the nerve itself is damaged → BOTH upper and lower face paralyzed on the same side.

follow_ups.question: How do you test this clinically?
follow_ups.answer: 'Raise your eyebrows' (tests frontalis) — equal in UMN, asymmetric in LMN. 'Show your teeth' (tests lower face) — asymmetric in both. UMN: eyebrows equal, lower face asymmetric. LMN: both asymmetric on same side.

follow_ups.question: What is Bell's phenomenon?
follow_ups.answer: When asked to close eyes, the eyeball rolls upward and outward (normal protective reflex). Visible in LMN facial palsy because the eye cannot close fully, revealing the upward rolling. Not a pathological sign itself — it's a normal reflex made visible by the palsy.

vivas.question: How do you differentiate cortical from capsular hemiplegia?
vivas.answer: Cortical (MCA): face + arm > leg (or leg > arm if ACA), cortical signs present (aphasia, neglect, astereognosis, agraphesthesia), homonymous hemianopia, eyes deviate TOWARDS lesion, seizures more common. Capsular: equal face = arm = leg (dense pure motor hemiplegia), NO cortical signs, variable hemianopia. Internal capsule: motor fibers densely packed in posterior limb → small lesion causes devastating equal hemiplegia.

follow_ups.question: What is the blood supply of the internal capsule?
follow_ups.answer: Anterior limb: medial striate artery (ACA), recurrent artery of Heubner. Genu: direct ICA branches. Posterior limb: lateral striate (lenticulostriate) arteries from MCA — 'arteries of stroke' — small penetrating end-arteries vulnerable to hypertensive lipohyalinosis. Charcot's 'artery of cerebral hemorrhage' is a lateral striate artery.

vivas.question: Name the crossed brainstem syndromes.
vivas.answer: Weber syndrome (midbrain ventral): ipsilateral CN3 palsy + contralateral hemiplegia. Benedikt syndrome (midbrain tegmentum): CN3 + contralateral tremor/ataxia (red nucleus) + hemiplegia. Millard-Gubler (pons ventral): ipsilateral CN6 + CN7 (LMN) + contralateral hemiplegia. Foville (pons tegmentum): Millard-Gubler + lateral gaze palsy (PPRF) — eyes deviate AWAY from lesion. Wallenberg (lateral medulla): NOT a crossed hemiplegia — ipsilateral Horner, facial pain/temp loss, cerebellar ataxia, palate/vocal cord palsy + contralateral body pain/temp loss. PICA occlusion.

follow_ups.question: Why are brainstem syndromes 'crossed'?
follow_ups.answer: Cranial nerve nuclei and their fascicles are at the level of the brainstem → ipsilateral LMN palsy. The corticospinal tract hasn't decussated yet (decussation at lower medulla pyramidal decussation) → contralateral hemiplegia. The cranial nerve palsy localizes the LEVEL.

vivas.question: Explain the mechanism of Babinski sign.
vivas.answer: The plantar response is a spinal reflex. Normally, the corticospinal tract INHIBITS the flexion-withdrawal reflex component that extends the big toe. When the corticospinal tract is damaged (UMN lesion), this inhibition is lost → the primitive flexion-withdrawal reflex re-emerges → big toe extends (dorsiflexes) as part of the withdrawal pattern. 'Extensor plantar' means the big toe dorsiflexes — in anatomical terms this IS extension of the hallux, but functionally it is part of the FLEXION withdrawal reflex (the whole limb flexes).

follow_ups.question: What is the difference between spasticity and rigidity?
follow_ups.answer: Spasticity (UMN/pyramidal): velocity-DEPENDENT, clasp-knife (resistance increases then suddenly gives way), affects antigravity muscles predominantly (flexors in arm, extensors in leg). Rigidity (extrapyramidal/basal ganglia): velocity-INDEPENDENT, lead-pipe or cogwheel (uniform resistance throughout range), affects ALL muscle groups equally in all directions.

vivas.question: What is the CHA2DS2-VASc score and how does it guide anticoagulation?
vivas.answer: CHA2DS2-VASc: Congestive heart failure (1), Hypertension (1), Age ≥75 (2), Diabetes (1), Stroke/TIA/thromboembolism (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex category female (1). Score 0: no anticoagulation. Score 1 in males/2 in females: consider anticoagulation. Score ≥2 in males/≥3 in females: anticoagulate. This patient scores at least 4 (HTN 1 + DM 1 + Stroke 2) — definite anticoagulation indication.

follow_ups.question: Why are DOACs preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF?
follow_ups.answer: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) have predictable pharmacokinetics (no INR monitoring), fewer drug/food interactions, rapid onset, similar/lower bleeding risk, and similar/better stroke prevention vs warfarin. Exceptions: valvular AF (RHD/mechanical valve) → warfarin only. APS → warfarin only (TRAPS trial showed DOACs inferior).

presentation_script.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a young male who is wheelchair-bound with an indwelling urethral catheter.
presentation_script.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is emaciated, febrile with low-grade temperature, has grade 3 pressure sores over the sacrum, and is catheterized. No pallor, icterus, cyanosis, or clubbing.
presentation_script.system_findings: On spine examination, there is a gibbus deformity (angular kyphosis) at the T8-T9 vertebral level with tenderness over spinous processes T7-T10 and paraspinal muscle spasm. Motor examination of both lower limbs reveals spasticity with clasp-knife tone, bilateral sustained ankle clonus, power of 1/5 at hips and knees and 2/5 at ankles bilaterally, with upper limbs 5/5. Deep tendon reflexes are exaggerated bilaterally at knees and ankles (3+) with sustained clonus. Plantar response is extensor bilaterally (bilateral Babinski positive). Superficial abdominal reflexes are absent below T10. Sensory examination shows complete loss of all modalities below T10 (umbilicus) bilaterally with a sharply demarcated sensory level. The patient has neurogenic bladder with catheter in situ.
presentation_script.diagnosis_statement: This patient has UMN-type paraplegia with T10 sensory level secondary to Pott's spine (spinal tuberculosis) causing cord compression at the thoracic level, as evidenced by gibbus deformity, spastic paraplegia, bilateral hyperreflexia, bilateral Babinski, complete sensory loss below T10, and neurogenic bladder, in the setting of constitutional symptoms.
differentials: Pott's spine (spinal TB) — most likely given gibbus, constitutional symptoms, endemic area
Metastatic spinal cord compression (but age against it, no known primary)
Transverse myelitis (but gradual onset, gibbus favors TB, not acute/subacute)
Spinal cord tumor — meningioma/neurofibroma (gradual, but no gibbus)
Epidural abscess (but gibbus suggests chronic destruction, not acute abscess)
tips: Always state: Type (UMN/LMN) + Sensory level + Bladder/bowel involvement + Topographic diagnosis (cord level) + Etiological diagnosis + Supporting signs.
First determination the examiner expects: 'Sir, this is an UMN-type / LMN-type paraplegia.' Everything follows from this.
Paraplegia (power 0/5) vs paraparesis (power 1-4/5) — use the precise term based on your power grading.

case_scenario.chief_complaint: Progressive bilateral lower limb weakness with inability to walk for 4 months, urinary retention for 2 months
case_scenario.hpi: Patient noticed gradual onset bilateral lower limb weakness starting 4 months ago, initially difficulty climbing stairs, progressing to inability to stand or walk. Associated with back pain at T8-T9 level for 6 months preceding weakness. Urinary retention requiring catheterization for 2 months. Constitutional symptoms: low-grade fever, night sweats, weight loss of 8 kg over 6 months, loss of appetite.
case_scenario.past_history: No prior TB treatment. No known contact with TB. No history of trauma, malignancy, or autoimmune disease.
case_scenario.social_history: Manual laborer, low socioeconomic status, crowded living conditions

clinical_summary.detail: Low-grade fever, pressure sores sacrum (grade 3), catheterized, wheelchair-bound
clinical_summary.significance: Chronic illness with complications of immobility. Catheter = neurogenic bladder

clinical_summary.detail: Angular kyphosis at T8-T9, tenderness over T7-T10 spinous processes, paraspinal muscle spasm
clinical_summary.significance: Gibbus = vertebral body collapse (anterior wedging). Angular kyphosis = Pott's spine. Smooth kyphosis = Scheuermann's or osteoporotic

clinical_summary.detail: Clasp-knife spasticity both LL, bilateral sustained ankle clonus (>5 beats)
clinical_summary.significance: UMN lesion → spasticity. Bilateral = cord lesion. Sustained clonus = severe UMN dysfunction

clinical_summary.detail: Hips 1/5, knees 1/5, ankles 2/5 bilaterally. Upper limbs 5/5
clinical_summary.significance: Paraplegia (both LL, UL spared) = lesion below cervical cord. Roughly equal bilaterally = midline cord compression

clinical_summary.detail: Knee 3+, ankle 3+ bilaterally with sustained clonus. Abdominal reflexes absent below T10
clinical_summary.significance: UMN. Absent abdominal reflexes confirm cord level (upper T7-T9, lower T10-T12)

clinical_summary.detail: Bilateral extensor plantar response
clinical_summary.significance: Bilateral Babinski = bilateral corticospinal tract involvement = cord lesion

clinical_summary.detail: All modalities (light touch, pinprick, vibration, JPS) lost below umbilicus bilaterally. Sharp demarcation
clinical_summary.significance: Complete transverse cord lesion → all tracts interrupted. T10 = umbilicus landmark

clinical_summary.detail: Catheterized. Initial retention → reflex bladder (automatic emptying with residual)
clinical_summary.significance: UMN bladder: loss of voluntary control. Initially retention (spinal shock), then reflex bladder. LMN bladder: atonic, overflow incontinence

examination_technique.sequence: General Inspection → Spine Examination → Motor (Tone → Power → Bulk) → Sensory Level → Reflexes & Plantar → Bladder Assessment

actions: Posture: legs extended (UMN) vs frog-leg (LMN). Catheter, pressure sores, wheelchair, orthoses.
Wasting: symmetric or asymmetric. Fasciculations: watch 30 seconds.
Constitutional signs: emaciation, febrile, lymphadenopathy.
Inspection: gibbus (angular kyphosis = Pott's), smooth kyphosis, scoliosis, scars, sinuses.
Palpation: run fingers down spinous processes — tenderness identifies level. Paraspinal spasm. Step deformity.
Percussion: tap each spinous process with hammer — localized pain at pathology level.
Gibbus: sharp angulation = vertebral body collapse (TB, fracture). Smooth curve = degenerative/postural.
Tone: passive movements at hip, knee, ankle. Spasticity (clasp-knife) vs hypotonia.
Power: test each myotome — hip flexion (L1-2), hip extension (L5-S1), knee extension (L3-4), knee flexion (L5-S1), ankle dorsiflexion (L4-5), ankle plantarflexion (S1-2), great toe extension (L5).
Wasting: compare sides. Measure thigh circumference 15cm above medial knee.
Technique: start from insensate area (below lesion) and move UPWARD until patient reports change. Repeat from normal area DOWNWARD. Two points should converge at same dermatome.
Test modalities separately: pinprick (spinothalamic), light touch, vibration (dorsal column — 128Hz tuning fork), joint position sense.
Dissociated sensory loss: pain/temp lost with preserved proprioception → anterior cord. Proprioception lost with preserved pain → posterior cord.
Sacral sparing: perianal sensation preserved = incomplete injury (central cord pattern) — CRITICAL prognostic sign.
findings_in_this_case: Complete loss all modalities below T10 (umbilicus)

actions: DTR: knee (L3-4), ankle (S1-2). Compare sides.
Superficial: abdominal reflexes (T7-T12) — absent below cord level. Cremasteric (L1-2). Anal reflex (S4-5).
Plantar: Babinski technique. Extensor = UMN.
Beevor's sign: flex neck, observe umbilicus. Moves upward = lower rectus weak (T10 level cord lesion).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: TB bacilli (hematogenous spread from primary focus) infect anterior vertebral body → caseating granulomatous inflammation → bone destruction → anterior wedge collapse of vertebral bodies → angular kyphosis
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: TB hematogenous spread → anterior vertebral body infection → caseation + bone destruction → anterior wedge collapse → angular kyphosis (gibbus)
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: TB destroys disc space (unlike metastases which SPARE disc space — a key distinguishing MRI feature). Smooth kyphosis = not TB (degenerative or Scheuermann's).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Cord compression by TB mass/abscess/vertebral collapse → bilateral corticospinal tract damage → bilateral UMN signs including Babinski
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Vertebral collapse → cord compression → bilateral corticospinal tracts damaged → bilateral extensor plantar
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Bilateral Babinski = bilateral corticospinal tract involvement = cord lesion. Unilateral Babinski = hemisphere or unilateral cord (Brown-Sequard).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Complete transverse cord compression interrupts all ascending sensory tracts (spinothalamic + dorsal columns) at the lesion level → complete sensory loss below
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Cord compression at T8-T9 vertebral level → sensory tracts interrupted → sensory level at T10 (cord segment is 1-2 levels below vertebral level in thoracic region)
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: The cord segment is always HIGHER than the vertebral segment in the thoracic region. Cord ends at L1 vertebra. T10 sensory level → lesion at approximately T8 cord segment / T6-T7 vertebral level.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Cord lesion above conus (S2-S4) disrupts descending voluntary control of micturition. Initially: retention (spinal shock → detrusor areflexia). Later: reflex/spastic bladder (automatic emptying at low volumes, urgency, incontinence)
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Cord lesion above conus → loss of voluntary micturition control → initially retention (spinal shock) → later reflex bladder (detrusor hyperreflexia + detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia)
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: LMN bladder (conus/cauda lesion) = atonic, large capacity, overflow incontinence, no urgency. The distinction determines catheter management strategy.

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: UMN-type paraplegia with T10 sensory level secondary to Pott's spine (spinal tuberculosis) causing cord compression at thoracic level
supporting_evidence: Gibbus deformity at T8-T9 (angular kyphosis = vertebral body collapse)
UMN signs: spasticity, hyperreflexia, bilateral Babinski, clonus
Complete sensory level at T10
Neurogenic bladder with early retention
Constitutional symptoms: fever, weight loss, night sweats
Young male in TB-endemic area
Progressive course over months (not acute)

differentials.points_for: Progressive course, sensory level, bladder involvement
differentials.why_not: Gibbus favors TB. Tumors usually cause pain worse at night/lying down. No weight loss expected in benign tumors
Usually acute/subacute (days, not months). No gibbus. No constitutional symptoms. Often post-infectious
Abscess is usually acute/subacute. Gibbus suggests chronic vertebral destruction rather than acute abscess

differentials.points_for: Can cause longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis
differentials.why_not: Usually relapsing course with optic neuritis. No gibbus. Predominantly affects women
Young age (metastases more common >50). No known primary. TB far more common at this age in India. MRI: TB destroys disc, metastases spare disc

investigations.expected: Anterior vertebral body destruction, paravertebral abscess (psoas abscess may extend to groin), cord compression, disc involvement. TB destroys disc; metastasis SPARES disc — key distinguishing feature
investigations.why: Gold standard imaging for cord compression and etiology

investigations.expected: Vertebral collapse, reduced disc space, gibbus, erosion. Vertebra plana (complete collapse)
investigations.why: First investigation. Shows bony destruction

investigations.expected: Markedly elevated (>50mm/hr)
investigations.why: Non-specific but useful for monitoring treatment response

investigations.expected: Often strongly positive (>15mm). Negative does NOT rule out TB (anergy in disseminated TB)
investigations.why: Supportive evidence for TB

investigations.expected: Caseating granulomas, AFB stain positive, culture positive
investigations.why: Gold standard for histopathological confirmation when clinical picture is unclear

investigations.expected: Detects MTB DNA + rifampicin resistance in 2 hours
investigations.why: Rapid molecular test on biopsy tissue

investigations.expected: 30-50% have concurrent pulmonary TB
investigations.why: Screen for pulmonary TB

investigations.expected: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein in TB. Also useful if TM/NMO/MS suspected
investigations.why: If non-compressive cause needs exclusion

medical.rationale: Treat the underlying TB infection and reduce inflammation/compression
medical.specifics: Category I: 2HRZE/4HR (2 months intensive: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol → 4 months continuation: HR). Total 9-12 months for skeletal TB (some extend to 18 months for extensive disease). Extended due to poor drug penetration into avascular necrotic bone and caseous tissue.

Pressure sore care (air mattress, 2-hourly turning), bladder care (CIC > indwelling catheter), DVT prophylaxis (LMWH + compression), nutritional support, physiotherapy (passive ROM, prevent contractures)

management.interventional: CT-guided biopsy for histopathological confirmation if diagnosis uncertain
management.surgical: Indications: progressive neurological deficit despite adequate ATT (4-6 weeks), severe cord compression with rapid deterioration, large epidural abscess, severe kyphotic deformity (>50 degrees) with instability. Procedures: anterior decompression + fusion (debridement of caseous tissue + bone grafting + instrumented fixation)
management.this_patient: Start ATT immediately (do not wait for biopsy if clinical picture is classic). Monitor neurological status weekly. Supportive care for pressure sores and bladder. Surgical consultation if no improvement in 4-6 weeks.
management.follow_up: Monthly neurological assessment, ESR monitoring for treatment response, MRI at 3 and 6 months, compliance monitoring (DOTS), assess for drug side effects (hepatotoxicity, optic neuritis with ethambutol)

complications.mechanism: Caseating material tracks along fascial planes from vertebral body → forms collection in psoas sheath → may present as groin swelling
complications.presentation: Fluctuant groin swelling, back pain, fever. May track to thigh
complications.management: CT-guided drainage + ATT. Surgical drainage if large

complications.mechanism: Ongoing vertebral body destruction → increasing angular kyphosis → cosmetic and functional impairment
complications.presentation: Visible gibbus, back pain, potential respiratory compromise if severe
complications.management: ATT to halt disease. Surgical correction and stabilization if >50 degrees

complications.mechanism: Immobility + sensory loss → sustained pressure on bony prominences → tissue ischemia → necrosis
complications.management: Air mattress, 2-hourly turning, wound care, nutritional optimization

complications.mechanism: MDR-TB (resistant to INH + rifampicin) from inadequate treatment or primary resistance
complications.presentation: Failure to improve on standard ATT
complications.management: GeneXpert for rifampicin resistance. Modified regimen with second-line drugs (fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, linezolid)

vivas.question: How do you find and document the sensory level?
vivas.answer: Start from an area of NO sensation (below the lesion) and move UPWARD until the patient reports a change. Mark that point. Repeat from normal area DOWNWARD. The two points should converge at the same dermatome — this is the sensory level. Test modalities separately: pinprick (spinothalamic), vibration (dorsal column). Key landmarks: T4 = nipple, T6 = xiphisternum, T10 = umbilicus, L1 = inguinal ligament.

follow_ups.question: What is the significance of the cord-vertebral level discrepancy?
follow_ups.answer: The cord is shorter than the vertebral column (ends at L1). In thoracic region: cord segment = vertebral level + 2-3. So T10 sensory level corresponds to cord lesion at approximately T8 segment, which sits at T6-T7 vertebral level. This is why the gibbus at T8-T9 vertebra causes a T10 sensory level.

follow_ups.question: What is Beevor's sign?
follow_ups.answer: Ask patient to flex neck (chin to chest) while supine. Observe umbilicus. If it moves UPWARD → lower rectus abdominis weak (below T10) with preserved upper rectus (T7-T9). Localizes cord lesion to T10. Highly specific for T10 level cord lesion.

vivas.question: How do you differentiate UMN from LMN type bladder?
vivas.answer: UMN bladder (cord lesion above conus S2-S4): spastic, small capacity, reflex emptying, urgency, detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia, retention initially (spinal shock) then automatic bladder. LMN bladder (conus/cauda equina lesion): atonic, large capacity, overflow incontinence, no urgency, bladder expressible. UMN bladder retains residual urine due to dyssynergia; LMN bladder has continuous dribbling.

follow_ups.question: What is the management of neurogenic bladder?
follow_ups.answer: Clean intermittent catheterization (CIC) is preferred over indwelling catheter (lower infection risk). Anticholinergics (oxybutynin) for detrusor hyperreflexia after spinal shock resolves. Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) for sphincter dyssynergia. Suprapubic catheter if CIC not possible. Monitor for UTIs and renal function.

vivas.question: What are the cord syndromes and how do you distinguish them?
vivas.answer: Complete transverse: ALL motor + sensory + autonomic lost below level. Brown-Sequard (hemisection): ipsilateral UMN weakness + proprioception loss, contralateral pain/temperature loss. Anterior cord: bilateral motor + pain/temp loss, PRESERVED proprioception (posterior columns supplied by posterior spinal arteries — spared). Central cord: arms > legs weakness (cervical fibers medial, sacral lateral), cape-like dissociated sensory loss. Posterior cord: proprioception/vibration/discriminative touch loss, motor and pain/temp preserved.

follow_ups.question: What causes anterior spinal artery syndrome?
follow_ups.answer: Anterior spinal artery occlusion — the cord's 'stroke'. Causes: aortic surgery/clamping, aortic dissection, atherosclerosis, vasculitis. Presents: sudden onset bilateral motor loss + pain/temperature loss with PRESERVED proprioception. Worst prognosis of incomplete syndromes.

vivas.question: How do you differentiate compressive from non-compressive myelopathy?
vivas.answer: Compressive (extramedullary): back pain (often first symptom), localized tenderness, progressive course, radicular symptoms, MRI shows extrinsic compression (tumor, abscess, TB). Non-compressive (intramedullary/medical): acute/subacute onset, no localized spine tenderness, MRI shows cord signal change without compression (TM, NMO, MS, vascular). Key: compressive causes are potentially surgical emergencies requiring urgent decompression. The MRI differentiates — this is why urgent MRI is mandatory.

follow_ups.question: What MRI features distinguish TB from metastatic compression?
follow_ups.answer: TB: anterior vertebral body destruction, DISC SPACE INVOLVED (destroyed), paravertebral abscess (psoas), contiguous vertebral involvement, subligamentous spread. Metastases: vertebral body replaced BUT DISC SPACE SPARED (tumor does not cross disc), posterior elements often involved, multiple skip lesions, pedicle destruction early.

presentation_script.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 35-year-old male who is alert and cooperative, on continuous cardiac monitoring.
presentation_script.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is tachycardic at 110/min with labile blood pressure (160/90 supine → 100/60 standing), respiratory rate 22/min, using accessory muscles of respiration. No pallor, icterus, cyanosis, or clubbing.
presentation_script.system_findings: Cranial nerve examination reveals bilateral LMN facial palsy — cannot close eyes, cannot smile, cannot puff cheeks bilaterally — with nasal speech and weak palatal movement. Motor examination shows marked flaccid hypotonia in all four limbs. Power: neck flexion 3/5, deltoids 2/5, biceps 3/5, grip 3/5, hip flexors 2/5, knee extension 2/5, ankle dorsiflexion 1/5 — symmetric, with distal > proximal weakness. ALL deep tendon reflexes are absent bilaterally (generalized areflexia). Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally. Sensory examination: subjective paresthesias in hands and feet but intact objective sensation. Respiratory assessment: single breath count 15, neck flexion 3/5, accessory muscle use.
presentation_script.diagnosis_statement: This patient has Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP variant) with respiratory compromise, bilateral facial palsy, and autonomic instability, preceded by Campylobacter enteritis, as evidenced by acute ascending symmetric flaccid quadriparesis, generalized areflexia, minimal sensory signs, bilateral LMN facial palsy, and autonomic dysfunction.
differentials: GBS (AIDP) — ascending paralysis, areflexia, preceding infection, bilateral facial palsy
Transverse myelitis (but areflexia + flaccidity + no sensory level + no bladder = LMN, not UMN)
Myasthenia gravis (but MG has fluctuating/fatigable weakness, preserved reflexes, ptosis/diplopia)
Acute poliomyelitis (but polio is ASYMMETRIC, pure motor, fever at onset)
Hypokalemic paralysis (but no sensory symptoms, rapid recovery with K+)
Botulism (DESCENDING, not ascending — starts cranial nerves → arms → legs)
tips: State: Onset pattern (acute, ascending) + LMN type + Distribution (quadriparesis) + Areflexia + Cranial nerve involvement + Autonomic features + Preceding illness + Specific variant.
Areflexia is the HALLMARK — present from early. If reflexes are brisk → NOT GBS, think cord lesion.
Always assess respiratory function (FVC, single breath count, neck flexion) — this is the life-threatening component.

case_scenario.chief_complaint: Progressive weakness of all four limbs for 5 days, bilateral facial weakness for 3 days, difficulty breathing for 1 day
case_scenario.hpi: Patient developed tingling in feet 5 days ago, followed by ascending weakness starting in feet → legs → arms over 5 days. Bilateral facial weakness noticed 3 days ago. Difficulty breathing, palpitations, and dizziness on standing for 1 day. History of diarrheal illness (watery diarrhea, abdominal cramps) 2 weeks prior which resolved spontaneously in 3 days. No fever, no headache, no bladder symptoms initially.
case_scenario.past_history: No prior neurological illness. No diabetes, hypertension.

clinical_summary.detail: Tachycardia 110/min resting, BP labile (160/90 supine → 100/60 standing), RR 22/min
clinical_summary.significance: Autonomic dysfunction in 70% of GBS. Tachycardia, BP fluctuations, arrhythmias — can be LIFE-THREATENING

clinical_summary.detail: Cannot close eyes, smile, or puff cheeks BILATERALLY. Nasal speech. Weak palatal movement
clinical_summary.significance: LMN facial palsy (nerve affected, not UMN pathway). Bilateral = GBS pattern (Bell's palsy is typically unilateral). Bulbar weakness → aspiration risk

clinical_summary.detail: All four limbs markedly flaccid
clinical_summary.significance: LMN → loss of alpha motor neuron drive → flaccidity. Distinguishes from cord lesion (spasticity)

clinical_summary.detail: Neck flexion 3/5. Deltoids 2/5. Biceps 3/5. Grip 3/5. Hip flexors 2/5. Knee extension 2/5. Ankle dorsiflexion 1/5
clinical_summary.significance: Symmetric, ascending pattern. All four limbs = quadriparesis. MRC sum score for monitoring

clinical_summary.detail: ALL DTRs absent bilaterally (biceps, triceps, supinator, knee, ankle)
clinical_summary.significance: Generalized areflexia is the HALLMARK of GBS. Due to interruption of reflex arc at peripheral nerve level

clinical_summary.detail: Flexor or absent/mute bilaterally
clinical_summary.significance: NO extensor plantar = NO UMN involvement. RULES OUT cord lesion

clinical_summary.detail: Subjective paresthesias hands/feet. Intact light touch, pinprick, vibration, JPS throughout
clinical_summary.significance: Sensory SYMPTOMS with minimal/absent sensory SIGNS = classic GBS

clinical_summary.detail: Single breath count 15 (normal >20). Neck flexion 3/5. Accessory muscles in use
clinical_summary.significance: Respiratory muscle involvement = MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Monitor FVC serially. FVC <20 mL/kg → consider intubation

examination_technique.sequence: Vitals + Autonomic → Respiratory Assessment → Cranial Nerves → Motor (Tone → Power) → Reflexes & Plantar → Sensory

actions: HR variability: tachycardia then sudden bradycardia.
BP supine and standing: postural drop >20 systolic.
Continuous cardiac monitoring should be in place.
Respiratory rate, single breath count (normal >20, <15 = significant, <10 = imminent failure).
Neck flexion power (shares C3-C5 innervation with diaphragm — neck flexion <4/5 = diaphragm likely weakening).
Look for accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing (abdomen IN during inspiration = diaphragm paralysis).
Do NOT rely on SpO2/ABG alone — by the time these are abnormal, patient is in extremis. FVC is the sensitive early indicator.
Facial: bilateral LMN weakness (cannot close eyes, wrinkle forehead, smile — ALL of face affected bilaterally).
Bulbar: gag reflex, palatal movement, nasal speech, secretion pooling.
Eye movements: check for MFS overlap (ophthalmoplegia).
Power: grade all major muscle groups. MRC sum score (6 groups bilaterally, total /60). Score <40 = severe. <48 at admission = predictor of ventilation need.
Tone: flaccid throughout.
Reflexes: test ALL — generalized areflexia expected. Early preservation does NOT exclude GBS.
Plantar: flexor or mute. NEVER extensor (extensor = wrong diagnosis).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Molecular mimicry: Campylobacter jejuni surface gangliosides resemble peripheral nerve gangliosides → immune antibodies attack nerve myelin (AIDP) or axonal membrane (AMAN) → conduction block/failure → weakness starting distally (longest nerves affected first) and ascending
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Campylobacter infection → molecular mimicry → anti-ganglioside antibodies → peripheral nerve demyelination/axonal damage → ascending paralysis
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Not all GBS is post-Campylobacter. Other triggers: CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, influenza, Zika, COVID-19 vaccination (rare). In 30% no trigger is identified.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Demyelination of peripheral nerves interrupts the afferent and/or efferent limb of the monosynaptic stretch reflex arc → reflexes lost early
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Peripheral nerve demyelination → reflex arc interrupted at nerve level → areflexia
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Occasional early preservation of reflexes does NOT exclude GBS (may still be evolving). Areflexia develops within days in most patients.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: GBS affects peripheral nerves including cranial nerves. Facial nerve is commonly involved (50%). Bilateral involvement is characteristic of GBS (unlike Bell's palsy which is unilateral)
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Immune attack on facial nerve myelin bilaterally → bilateral LMN facial weakness (entire face affected on both sides)
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Unilateral facial palsy in the context of GBS can occur but is less typical. Always check for bilateral involvement.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Immune attack on autonomic nerve fibers → both sympathetic and parasympathetic dysfunction → tachycardia, BP fluctuations, arrhythmias
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Autoimmune attack → autonomic nerve demyelination → sympathetic/parasympathetic imbalance → labile HR and BP
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Autonomic dysfunction causes most GBS deaths (arrhythmias, sudden asystole). Avoid triggers: aggressive suctioning, bladder distension. Have atropine at bedside.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Inflammation at nerve root level → blood-nerve barrier breakdown → protein leaks into CSF. But inflammation is at the nerve, NOT in the meninges → no cellular infiltration into CSF
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Nerve root inflammation → blood-nerve barrier breakdown → protein leaks into CSF → elevated protein with NORMAL cell count
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: CSF may be NORMAL in the first week (protein rises over 1-2 weeks). Normal LP in first 3-5 days does NOT exclude GBS. If cell count >50 → think HIV, Lyme, sarcoid.

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP variant) with respiratory compromise, bilateral facial palsy, and autonomic instability, preceded by Campylobacter enteritis
supporting_evidence: Ascending symmetric flaccid paralysis (feet → legs → arms over days)
Generalized areflexia (hallmark)
Bilateral LMN facial palsy
Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP)
Preceding diarrheal illness 2 weeks prior (Campylobacter)
Minimal sensory signs despite sensory symptoms
Flexor plantar responses (NO UMN signs)

differentials.why_not: GBS has areflexia + flaccidity; myelitis has UMN signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski). Myelitis has sensory LEVEL. Early bladder in myelitis. TRAP: acute myelitis spinal shock mimics GBS — but myelitis has sensory level and early bladder.
MG has fluctuating/fatigable weakness, preserved reflexes, ptosis + diplopia (not typical of GBS), NO sensory symptoms. MG is NOT ascending.

differentials.points_for: Acute flaccid paralysis, preceding viral illness
differentials.why_not: Polio is ASYMMETRIC, affects lower limbs preferentially, fever at onset, PURE motor (no sensory at all).
No sensory symptoms, rapid recovery with K+ replacement, no facial involvement, serum K+ low.
Botulism is DESCENDING (cranial → arms → legs — OPPOSITE of GBS). Pupillary dilatation. Contaminated food history.

investigations.expected: Albuminocytologic dissociation: elevated protein (>0.45 g/L, often 1-10 g/L) with NORMAL cell count (<10 cells/uL). May be normal in first week — repeat at 1 week if high suspicion.
investigations.why: The hallmark CSF finding. If cell count >50 → consider HIV, Lyme, sarcoid

investigations.expected: AIDP: prolonged distal latencies, slowed conduction velocities (<70% LLN), conduction block, temporal dispersion, prolonged/absent F-waves. AMAN: reduced CMAP amplitudes, normal velocities, normal sensory potentials.
investigations.why: Distinguishes AIDP from AMAN — affects prognosis. May be normal in first 1-2 weeks; earliest finding is absent F-waves.

investigations.expected: FVC declining. Intubate if: FVC <20 mL/kg or <1L, MIP <-30 cmH2O, MEP <40 cmH2O, FVC declining >30%
investigations.why: The single most important bedside investigation. Do NOT rely on SpO2/ABG — by the time abnormal, patient is in extremis

investigations.expected: Anti-GQ1b (MFS >90%), anti-GM1/GD1a (AMAN). Not required for diagnosis but helpful for variant classification
investigations.why: Variant classification and prognosis

investigations.expected: Normal K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+
investigations.why: Rule out hypokalemic paralysis (a mimic)

investigations.expected: May confirm preceding Campylobacter infection
investigations.why: Confirms trigger. PCR more sensitive than culture

investigations.expected: Negative (HIV can cause GBS-like picture with CSF pleocytosis)
investigations.why: Exclude HIV-associated GBS

medical.rationale: Immunomodulation — neutralizes pathogenic antibodies, modulates complement, downregulates immune attack
medical.specifics: Total 2 g/kg over 5 days. Check IgA levels before first infusion (anaphylaxis risk in IgA-deficient patients). Side effects: headache, aseptic meningitis, renal failure, thromboembolism.

medical.rationale: Physical removal of pathogenic antibodies, complement, and cytokines from circulation
medical.specifics: 5 exchanges over 2 weeks (200-250 mL/kg total). Contraindications: hemodynamic instability, sepsis. Equivalent efficacy to IVIg. Do NOT combine IVIg + PLEX (no additional benefit).

medical.rationale: Prevent complications during paralysis and recovery
medical.specifics: DVT prophylaxis (LMWH + compression), pain management (gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain), NG feeding if bulbar weakness, passive ROM physiotherapy from day 1, continuous cardiac monitoring, atropine at bedside for bradycardia, psychological support.

management.interventional: Elective intubation and mechanical ventilation if FVC <20 mL/kg or <1L, or rapid decline. Do NOT wait for respiratory arrest.
management.this_patient: This patient needs ICU admission (respiratory compromise + autonomic instability + rapid progression). Start IVIg immediately. Serial FVC every 4 hours. Low threshold for intubation. Continuous cardiac monitoring. DVT prophylaxis. Pain management.
management.follow_up: Serial neurological assessment (MRC sum score). Respiratory weaning protocol after improvement. Rehabilitation: progressive mobilization as power improves. 80% walk independently at 6 months. 5-10% have significant residual disability. Mortality 3-5%.

complications.mechanism: Ascending paralysis reaches diaphragm (C3-C5) and intercostal muscles → ventilatory failure
complications.presentation: Declining FVC, paradoxical breathing, inability to cough, hypoxia (late sign)
complications.management: Elective intubation. 20/30/40 rule. Do NOT rely on SpO2.

complications.mechanism: Sympathetic and parasympathetic nerve involvement → unopposed surges
complications.presentation: Sudden bradycardia/asystole, severe hypertension, cardiac arrhythmias
complications.management: Continuous monitoring, atropine for bradycardia, short-acting antihypertensives, avoid triggers (suctioning, bladder distension)

complications.presentation: Sudden dyspnea, chest pain, hemodynamic collapse
complications.management: LMWH prophylaxis, compression stockings, early mobilization

complications.mechanism: Nerve inflammation and damage → central sensitization
complications.presentation: Severe back pain, limb pain, paresthesias. Often undertreated
complications.management: Gabapentin/pregabalin first-line. Carbamazepine or tricyclics second-line.

vivas.question: What is albuminocytologic dissociation and why does it occur in GBS?
vivas.answer: Elevated CSF protein with NORMAL cell count. In GBS, inflammation is at the nerve ROOT level → blood-nerve barrier breakdown → protein leaks into CSF. But the inflammation is at the nerve, NOT in the meninges → no cellular infiltration into CSF. Contrast with meningitis where both cells AND protein rise. Other causes: spinal cord tumor (Froin's syndrome), CIDP, diabetic radiculoplexopathy.

follow_ups.question: When might CSF be normal in GBS?
follow_ups.answer: In the first 3-5 days. Protein rises over 1-2 weeks, peaks at 4-6 weeks. A normal CSF in week 1 does NOT exclude GBS — repeat at 1 week.

follow_ups.question: When should you suspect an alternative diagnosis from CSF?
follow_ups.answer: If cell count >50 cells/uL → consider HIV-associated GBS, Lyme disease, CMV polyradiculitis, lymphomatous meningitis, sarcoidosis. These cause GBS-like picture WITH pleocytosis.

vivas.question: How do you differentiate GBS from transverse myelitis at the bedside?
vivas.answer: GBS: LMN (flaccid, areflexic, flexor plantar ALWAYS), ascending pattern, sensory symptoms but NO sensory LEVEL, bladder late/mild, bilateral facial palsy common, CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation. TM: UMN (after spinal shock — spastic, hyperreflexic, Babinski positive), NOT ascending, SENSORY LEVEL (sharp cutoff), bladder EARLY and severe, no facial weakness, CSF shows pleocytosis. TRAP: acute myelitis has spinal shock initially (flaccid, areflexic) mimicking GBS. Key differentiator: TM has sensory LEVEL and early bladder even during spinal shock.

vivas.question: What is Miller Fisher syndrome?
vivas.answer: A variant of GBS with the triad: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + areflexia, WITHOUT limb weakness. Anti-GQ1b antibodies positive in >90%. GQ1b ganglioside is concentrated in oculomotor nerves (→ ophthalmoplegia) and muscle spindles (→ ataxia/areflexia). Excellent prognosis — most recover completely. May overlap with GBS (Fisher-GBS overlap — adding limb weakness).

follow_ups.question: What is Bickerstaff brainstem encephalitis?
follow_ups.answer: Ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + ALTERED CONSCIOUSNESS (unlike MFS where consciousness is normal). Anti-GQ1b positive. Overlaps with MFS on the GQ1b-antibody spectrum but involves CNS (brainstem) in addition to PNS.

vivas.question: Why don't corticosteroids work in GBS?
vivas.answer: Multiple RCTs show no benefit of steroids in GBS. GBS involves predominantly HUMORAL immunity (antibodies, complement) which steroids don't effectively suppress. This contrasts with CIDP where steroids DO work because CIDP involves more T-cell mediated immunity. Combined IVIg + steroids also show no benefit. This is a key exam point.

follow_ups.question: How does IVIg work in GBS?
follow_ups.answer: Multiple mechanisms: neutralization of pathogenic autoantibodies by anti-idiotypic antibodies, Fc receptor blockade on macrophages (reduces antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity), complement inhibition, modulation of cytokine production, acceleration of pathogenic IgG catabolism.

presentation_script.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 22-year-old female who is wheelchair-bound with a urinary catheter in situ.
presentation_script.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is afebrile, no skin rash, no joint swelling, no lymphadenopathy. Catheterized.
presentation_script.system_findings: Spine examination is normal — no gibbus, no tenderness, no deformity. Full range of neck movement, no Lhermitte's sign. Visual acuity is normal, no RAPD, fundoscopy normal — no optic neuritis. Motor examination shows evolving tone — initial hypotonia transitioning to spasticity (currently day 5). Power: both hips 1/5, knees 1/5, ankles 0/5 bilaterally. Upper limbs 5/5. Reflexes: knee and ankle jerks brisk 3+ bilaterally, abdominal reflexes absent below T6. Bilateral extensor plantar (Babinski positive). Sensory examination: complete loss of all modalities below T6 (xiphisternum) with band-like dysesthesia at T6 level. Bladder in retention (catheter draining 800mL).
presentation_script.diagnosis_statement: This patient has acute transverse myelitis (post-infectious, idiopathic), with complete transverse cord syndrome at T6 level, as evidenced by UMN paraplegia with hyperreflexia, bilateral Babinski, complete sensory loss below T6, neurogenic bladder with retention, acute onset over 48 hours following URI, and normal spine with no compressive pathology, pending AQP4/MOG antibodies and MRI characterization to exclude NMO and MS.
differentials: Idiopathic acute transverse myelitis (post-infectious — most likely given acute onset, preceding URI, no prior episodes)
NMO spectrum disorder (young female, severe complete cord syndrome — need AQP4 antibodies)
MS first presentation (young female, inflammatory myelitis — but MS rarely causes complete transverse syndrome)
Cord compression by tumor/abscess (but normal spine, no tenderness, MRI will differentiate)
Anterior spinal artery occlusion (but vascular is hyperacute minutes, not days)
tips: State: Age + sex + UMN type + Sensory level + Bladder + Temporal profile + Preceding illness + 'Transverse myelitis' + pending MRI/antibody characterization.
The critical distinction is: idiopathic ATM vs MS vs NMO. Getting this right determines long-term treatment. NMO drugs can worsen MS and vice versa.
In acute phase, spinal shock may produce flaccidity and areflexia — mimicking GBS. But sensory LEVEL and early bladder point to cord.

case_scenario.chief_complaint: Bilateral lower limb weakness progressing over 48 hours, band-like sensation around chest at T6 level, urinary retention
case_scenario.hpi: Patient developed band-like tightness around the chest (T6 level) 3 days ago, followed by rapidly progressive bilateral lower limb weakness over 48 hours — from mild difficulty walking to complete inability to stand. Urinary retention requiring catheterization within 24 hours of weakness onset. Had an upper respiratory infection (sore throat, coryza) 10 days prior which resolved. No visual symptoms, no prior similar episodes. No headache, no vomiting, no seizures.
case_scenario.past_history: No prior neurological episodes. No autoimmune disease. No family history of MS or NMO.

clinical_summary.detail: No fever, no rash, no joint swelling — less likely SLE or vasculitis
clinical_summary.significance: Catheter = neurogenic bladder. No systemic features to suggest connective tissue disease

clinical_summary.detail: No gibbus, no tenderness, no deformity. Full neck ROM. No Lhermitte's sign
clinical_summary.significance: Normal spine → non-compressive myelopathy. Absent Lhermitte's (though can be positive in MS/NMO with cervical involvement)

clinical_summary.detail: Visual acuity normal, no RAPD, fundoscopy normal
clinical_summary.significance: No optic neuritis — important for excluding NMO (which has optic neuritis as other hallmark)

clinical_summary.detail: Initial hypotonia (day 3 — spinal shock phase), now transitioning to spasticity (day 5)
clinical_summary.significance: Acute cord lesion → initial flaccidity (spinal shock). UMN signs emerge as shock resolves over days-weeks

clinical_summary.detail: Hips 1/5, knees 1/5, ankles 0/5 bilaterally. UL 5/5
clinical_summary.significance: Thoracic level cord lesion (LL involved, UL spared)

clinical_summary.detail: Knee 3+, ankle 3+ bilaterally. Abdominals absent below T6
clinical_summary.significance: UMN pattern emerging as spinal shock resolves

clinical_summary.detail: Bilateral extensor plantar
clinical_summary.significance: Bilateral corticospinal tract involvement = cord lesion

clinical_summary.detail: Complete loss all modalities below T6 (xiphisternum). Band-like dysesthesia at T6 level
clinical_summary.significance: Complete transverse myelopathy at T6. Band dysesthesia = posterior column/dorsal root entry zone involvement at lesion level

clinical_summary.detail: Catheter draining 800mL. No voluntary voiding
clinical_summary.significance: Cord lesion disrupting descending voluntary micturition control. Initially atonic (spinal shock), will become spastic UMN bladder

examination_technique.sequence: Confirm cord syndrome → Define sensory level → Characterize completeness → Look for extra-spinal clues → Spine examination

actions: Bilateral motor + sensory + bladder involvement with a sensory level = CORD.
Demonstrate UMN signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski). If very acute (spinal shock) → demonstrate flaccidity and state you expect UMN signs to emerge.
Key distinction even during spinal shock: sensory LEVEL and early bladder = cord, NOT GBS.
findings_in_this_case: Bilateral paraplegia + sensory level T6 + bladder retention = cord syndrome confirmed

actions: Pin from insensate area upward AND from normal area downward. Mark where they converge.
Key dermatome landmarks: T4 = nipple, T6 = xiphisternum, T10 = umbilicus.
findings_in_this_case: Complete loss all modalities below T6

actions: Complete transverse (all modalities lost) vs partial (dissociated sensory loss, asymmetric motor).
Complete → more likely NMO/idiopathic ATM. Partial → more likely MS.
Check sacral sparing (perianal sensation) — if preserved = incomplete injury.
findings_in_this_case: Complete transverse — all modalities lost bilaterally

actions: Eyes: RAPD (optic neuritis → NMO/MS), reduced acuity, disc pallor (previous optic neuritis).
Skin: butterfly rash (SLE), erythema nodosum (sarcoid), oral ulcers (Behcet).
Joints: synovitis (SLE, RA).
Salivary glands: enlargement (Sjogren).
findings_in_this_case: No RAPD, normal vision, normal fundi

actions: Rule out tenderness (compression), gibbus (TB).
Full neck movements — Lhermitte's sign (electric shock down spine on neck flexion → demyelination in posterior columns of cervical cord).
findings_in_this_case: No tenderness, no gibbus, no deformity

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Inflammation at the cord level involves posterior columns and dorsal root entry zone → abnormal sensory processing at the level of the lesion → constricting tightness sensation
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Cord inflammation at T6 → posterior column/dorsal root entry zone involvement → aberrant sensory processing → band sensation
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Band sensation/girdle pain is the CLASSIC early symptom of transverse myelitis. Often precedes motor weakness by hours-days. A patient with progressive band sensation should raise suspicion for myelitis.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Inflammation spans the entire WIDTH of the cord (hence 'transverse') → all tracts interrupted: corticospinal (motor), spinothalamic (pain/temp), dorsal columns (proprioception), autonomic (bladder)
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Inflammatory attack across cord width → all motor + sensory + autonomic tracts damaged → bilateral weakness + all-modality sensory loss + bladder retention
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Complete transverse syndrome → more likely NMO or idiopathic ATM. MS typically causes PARTIAL cord syndrome (asymmetric, one tract predominates).

findings_with_mechanisms.finding: Spinal shock transitioning to UMN signs
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Acute cord lesion → sudden loss of all descending input to spinal circuits → initial areflexia and flaccidity (spinal shock, duration days-weeks). As spinal circuits recover autonomous function → hyperexcitable reflexes emerge → spasticity + hyperreflexia + Babinski
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Acute cord lesion → spinal shock (flaccidity, areflexia) → spinal circuit recovery → UMN signs emerge (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski)
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Spinal shock in acute myelitis MIMICS GBS at the bedside. Key differentiator: myelitis has sensory LEVEL and early bladder even during spinal shock. GBS does not.

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: Acute transverse myelitis (post-infectious, idiopathic), with complete transverse cord syndrome at T6 level, pending AQP4/MOG antibodies and MRI characterization to exclude NMO and MS
supporting_evidence: Acute onset over 48 hours (within 4h-21d inflammatory window)
Bilateral motor + sensory + autonomic (bladder) involvement
Clearly defined sensory level at T6
Post-infectious trigger (URI 10 days prior)
No compressive pathology on spine exam
Young female — classic demographic

differentials.points_for: Young female, severe complete cord syndrome (NMO tends to be severe)
differentials.why_not: No optic neuritis, first episode. But NMO can present as isolated myelitis first. Need AQP4-Ab urgently. If LETM (>=3 segments) on MRI → high suspicion for NMO.
MS typically causes SHORT-segment (<2 vertebrae), PARTIAL cord syndrome (not complete transverse). MS rarely causes severe complete paraplegia at first presentation.
Normal spine exam (no tenderness, no gibbus). MRI will distinguish — URGENT MRI is mandatory to exclude this.
Vascular is hyperacute (seconds-minutes, not days). Produces anterior cord syndrome (motor + pain/temp lost, proprioception PRESERVED — not complete transverse).
No systemic SLE features (rash, joints, serositis). Need ANA/dsDNA.

investigations.expected: Cord swelling, T2 hyperintensity (edema), gadolinium enhancement (active inflammation). MUST exclude compression first. Count vertebral segments: <2 = short (MS), >=3 = LETM (NMO/MOG/idiopathic). Location: central grey matter = NMO, peripheral dorsolateral = MS.
investigations.why: First investigation — exclude compression (surgical emergency), then characterize the inflammatory lesion

investigations.expected: Look for MS lesions (Dawson's fingers, periventricular), NMO lesions (area postrema, periependymal), or normal brain (idiopathic ATM)
investigations.why: Critical for distinguishing MS vs NMO vs idiopathic ATM

investigations.expected: THE test for NMO. Sensitivity ~80%, specificity ~99%. Must use cell-based assay. Test BEFORE starting immunosuppression.
investigations.why: Positive = NMO spectrum disorder. Determines long-term treatment entirely.

investigations.expected: Test if AQP4 negative and clinical picture suggests NMO phenotype
investigations.why: MOG-AD overlaps with NMO phenotype. Different prognosis and treatment.

investigations.expected: Mild-moderate lymphocytic pleocytosis (10-200 cells), elevated protein (0.5-2 g/L), OCBs (present >95% MS, absent most NMO/ATM). IgG index elevated in MS.
investigations.why: OCBs are KEY distinguishing test between MS and NMO

investigations.expected: ANA, dsDNA, complement C3/C4 (SLE), anti-Ro/La (Sjogren), ACE level (sarcoid)
investigations.why: Exclude disease-associated myelitis

investigations.expected: Exclude infection-related myelitis
investigations.why: Infectious myelitis requires different treatment

medical.rationale: Reduces cord edema, suppresses immune attack, reduces blood-cord barrier permeability
medical.specifics: Start ASAP once infection and compression are excluded. Most patients improve within 3-5 days. Short oral taper 2-4 weeks in severe cases.

medical.rationale: Removes pathogenic antibodies. Strongest evidence in NMO-spectrum myelitis. Some centers use as first-line for suspected NMO.
medical.specifics: 5-7 exchanges over 10-14 days. When: no improvement after 5 days of methylprednisolone, or severe/worsening symptoms.

Idiopathic ATM (monophasic): no long-term treatment. NMO (AQP4+): rituximab, eculizumab, inebilizumab, satralizumab — NEVER MS drugs. MS: DMTs (ocrelizumab, natalizumab, etc.). MOG-AD if relapsing: steroids, mycophenolate, azathioprine.
CIC for bladder, DVT prophylaxis (LMWH), neuropathic pain management (gabapentin/pregabalin), spasticity (baclofen), physiotherapy, psychological support.

management.interventional: Surgical decompression if MRI shows compressive cause (must be excluded first)
management.this_patient: Start IV methylprednisolone 1g daily immediately after urgent MRI excludes compression. Send AQP4/MOG antibodies, CSF. If no response in 5 days → PLEX. Long-term treatment depends on antibody results and MRI characterization.
