management.follow_up: AQP4/MOG antibody results (determines long-term management). Repeat MRI at 3-6 months. Monitor for relapse (→ reclassify if recurs). Ophthalmology review (screen for subclinical optic neuritis). Rehabilitation.

complications.mechanism: First episode of myelitis may be the presenting feature of a relapsing condition
complications.presentation: Second episode of myelitis, or new optic neuritis, or brain lesions on follow-up MRI
complications.management: Reclassify and start appropriate long-term immunosuppression

complications.mechanism: Severe inflammatory damage → axonal loss (irreversible) + demyelination (potentially reversible)
complications.presentation: Persistent weakness, sensory loss, bladder dysfunction despite treatment

complications.mechanism: Cord damage → abnormal sensory processing → central neuropathic pain
complications.presentation: Burning, shooting pain below the level of lesion. Often chronic.

vivas.question: What are the TM Consortium Working Group 2002 diagnostic criteria?
vivas.answer: Inclusion: (1) sensory/motor/autonomic dysfunction attributable to cord, (2) bilateral signs, (3) defined sensory level, (4) inflammation demonstrated by CSF pleocytosis OR elevated IgG index OR gadolinium enhancement, (5) progression to nadir between 4 hours and 21 days. Exclusion: prior radiation, clear anterior spinal artery pattern, MRI evidence of compressive cause, connective tissue disease (classified as 'disease-associated'). Why time window: <4h = vascular, >21d = progressive myelopathy. After confirming ATM, classify as disease-associated (MS, NMO, SLE) or idiopathic (exclusion diagnosis).

vivas.question: What is LETM and why is it significant?
vivas.answer: LETM = Longitudinally Extensive Transverse Myelitis: cord lesion extending >=3 contiguous vertebral segments on MRI T2-weighted sagittal images. Significance: LETM is the hallmark MRI pattern of NMOSD. When you see LETM, AQP4 antibodies MUST be tested. MS rarely causes LETM (short-segment is the MS signature). Probability of NMO given LETM is ~50-70%. Other causes of LETM: MOG-AD, idiopathic ATM, SLE myelitis, sarcoid, infectious. NOT MS.

follow_ups.question: What is the area postrema syndrome?
follow_ups.answer: Area postrema: circumventricular organ at floor of 4th ventricle, LACKS blood-brain barrier, high AQP4 expression → accessible to circulating antibodies. Presents as intractable nausea/vomiting/hiccups unexplained by GI evaluation. Can be the FIRST manifestation of NMO — preceding optic neuritis or myelitis by months-years. Patient with unexplained intractable vomiting + normal GI workup → think NMO, check AQP4 antibodies.

vivas.question: How do you differentiate TM from GBS at the bedside?
vivas.answer: TM: UMN (after spinal shock — spastic, hyperreflexic, Babinski positive), NOT ascending, SENSORY LEVEL (sharp cutoff), bladder EARLY and severe, no facial weakness, CSF shows pleocytosis. GBS: LMN (flaccid, areflexic, flexor plantar ALWAYS), ASCENDING, NO sensory level, bladder late/mild, bilateral facial palsy common, CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation. The TRAP: acute myelitis (spinal shock) produces flaccidity and areflexia mimicking GBS. Key differentiator even then: TM has sensory LEVEL and early bladder retention.

vivas.question: Why is misdiagnosing NMO as MS dangerous?
vivas.answer: MS drugs (interferon-beta, fingolimod, natalizumab) can WORSEN NMO — triggering severe relapses. Conversely, NMO-specific treatments (rituximab, eculizumab) are highly effective and should not be withheld. NMO is astrocyte-primary (AQP4 on astrocyte foot processes → complement-mediated astrocyte destruction → secondary demyelination). MS is myelin-primary. Different pathology requires different treatment. The distinction hinges on: MRI pattern (LETM vs short-segment), antibodies (AQP4/MOG vs OCBs), and clinical features.

follow_ups.question: What is the role of OCBs?
follow_ups.answer: Oligoclonal bands in CSF = intrathecal IgG synthesis. Present in >95% of MS (highly sensitive). Absent in most NMO and idiopathic ATM. A KEY distinguishing test. OCB-positive CSF + short-segment myelitis + periventricular brain lesions = MS. OCB-negative CSF + LETM + AQP4 positive = NMO.

presentation_script.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 28-year-old female who is conscious and oriented.
presentation_script.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient has livedo reticularis on both lower limbs and a healed DVT scar on the left calf. No pallor, icterus, cyanosis, or clubbing. Pulse is regular.
presentation_script.system_findings: On neurological examination, there is left-sided neglect on double simultaneous stimulation. Left UMN facial palsy with lower face weakness and forehead sparing. Motor examination reveals spastic tone in left upper and lower limbs. Power is 2/5 in left upper limb (arm > hand) and 3/5 in left lower limb. Deep tendon reflexes are exaggerated on the left (3+). Plantar response is extensor on the left (Babinski positive), flexor on the right. Cortical sensory loss on the left: impaired two-point discrimination, astereognosis, extinction on double simultaneous stimulation. No aphasia (right hemisphere lesion in right-handed patient).
presentation_script.diagnosis_statement: This patient has right MCA territory ischemic stroke secondary to antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, evidenced by left UMN hemiplegia with face and arm > leg, cortical sensory loss, left-sided neglect, in a young female with recurrent pregnancy losses, prior DVT, and livedo reticularis — all pointing to a prothrombotic state.
differentials: APLA syndrome (recurrent abortions + DVT + livedo reticularis + stroke = classic APS)
SLE with secondary APS (young female, APS features may be secondary to SLE — need ANA/dsDNA)
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (young female, prothrombotic — but typical arterial territory pattern here)
Cardioembolism via PFO (young patient, paradoxical embolism — needs bubble echo)
Arterial dissection (young age — but no preceding neck trauma/pain/Horner)
Moyamoya disease (young female — but more common in East Asian)
tips: State: Age emphasis + UMN/LMN type + side + Vascular territory + Supporting signs + Young stroke etiology list + Clues pointing to specific cause.
Young stroke (<45 years) = different etiological spectrum. Do NOT default to atherosclerotic risk factors.
Always look for systemic clues: livedo reticularis, DVT scars, joint swelling, rash, absent pulses — these point to specific etiologies.

case_scenario.chief_complaint: Sudden onset left-sided weakness of body for 2 weeks
case_scenario.hpi: Patient woke up 2 weeks ago with sudden onset left-sided weakness involving face, arm, and leg, with arm weaker than leg. Associated left-sided inattention (bumping into objects on left side). No headache, vomiting, seizures, or loss of consciousness at onset. No conventional vascular risk factors (no HTN, DM, dyslipidemia, smoking).
case_scenario.past_history: History of 2 first-trimester spontaneous abortions (recurrent pregnancy loss). History of left leg deep vein thrombosis 2 years ago treated with warfarin for 6 months. No history of autoimmune disease diagnosed.
case_scenario.drug_history: No current medications (warfarin stopped after 6 months for DVT)

clinical_summary.detail: No aphasia (right hemisphere, non-dominant). Left-sided neglect. Livedo reticularis both LL. Healed DVT scar left calf
clinical_summary.significance: Right MCA territory. Non-dominant → neglect not aphasia. Livedo + DVT = APLA syndrome

clinical_summary.detail: Non-blanching reticular violaceous pattern on both lower limbs
clinical_summary.significance: Strongly associated with APS. Sneddon syndrome = livedo reticularis + cerebrovascular events

clinical_summary.detail: Lower face weak, forehead spared
clinical_summary.significance: Supranuclear lesion (right hemisphere) → contralateral lower face weak

clinical_summary.detail: Clasp-knife spasticity left UL and LL. Right side normal
clinical_summary.significance: UMN lesion, established (2 weeks)

clinical_summary.detail: Left UL 2/5 (arm > hand), left LL 3/5. Right 5/5
clinical_summary.significance: Face + arm > leg = MCA territory

clinical_summary.detail: 3+ at biceps, triceps, knee, ankle on left. Right normal (2+)
clinical_summary.significance: UMN pattern, unilateral = hemisphere lesion

clinical_summary.detail: Left extensor, right flexor
clinical_summary.significance: Confirms UMN lesion on left (right hemisphere)

clinical_summary.detail: Impaired two-point discrimination, astereognosis, extinction on double simultaneous stimulation
clinical_summary.significance: Parietal cortex involvement (right MCA territory)

examination_technique.sequence: Standard Hemiplegia Assessment + Systematic Search for Systemic Clues to Etiology

actions: Standard hemiplegia workup: tone, power, reflexes, plantar, sensory, gait, cranial nerves.
Key: identify vascular territory (MCA/ACA/PCA) and confirm UMN pattern.
findings_in_this_case: Right MCA territory: left hemiplegia, face + arm > leg, cortical sensory loss, neglect

actions: Skin: livedo reticularis (APLA), butterfly rash (SLE), angiokeratomas (Fabry), needle marks (IV drugs → IE), purpura (vasculitis/TTP).
Eyes: lens subluxation (homocystinuria/Marfan), corneal verticillata (Fabry), Roth spots (IE), Horner syndrome (carotid dissection).
Cardiovascular: ALL pulses (absent/asymmetric → Takayasu, dissection), BP both arms, murmurs (RHD, IE, PDA, MVP), carotid bruit.
Joints: arthropathy (SLE, RA → vasculitis), hypermobility (Ehlers-Danlos → dissection).
Abdomen: splenomegaly (IE, hematological malignancy).
findings_in_this_case: No rash, no joint swelling, no absent pulses

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Thrombosis of dermal arterioles → interrupted blood flow in watershed areas of skin → purple reticular (net-like) pattern representing deoxygenated blood in surrounding venules
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: APLA → dermal arteriolar thrombosis → interrupted blood flow → venular congestion → reticular purple pattern
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Also seen in cholesterol embolization, cryoglobulinemia, PAN, and physiological (cutis marmorata in cold). In context of stroke + DVT + abortions → APLA.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Antiphospholipid antibodies bind beta-2 glycoprotein I on endothelial cells → activate endothelium, platelets, and complement → prothrombotic state affecting BOTH arterial and venous beds
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: aPL antibodies → beta-2 GP1 binding → endothelial activation + platelet activation + complement activation → arterial (stroke) AND venous (DVT) thrombosis
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Combined arterial + venous thrombosis in the same patient is HIGHLY suggestive of APS. Most thrombophilias (protein C/S, Factor V Leiden) are predominantly venous.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: aPL antibodies → placental thrombosis + complement-mediated trophoblast injury → placental insufficiency → fetal loss
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: aPL → placental vessel thrombosis + trophoblast damage → placental insufficiency → first-trimester abortions or later stillbirth
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Recurrent pregnancy loss is a CLINICAL criterion for APS diagnosis (Sydney criteria). Must exclude other causes: chromosomal, anatomical, hormonal.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Right parietal cortex (non-dominant hemisphere) lesion → loss of attention to contralateral (left) space. The right hemisphere is dominant for spatial attention — left hemisphere cannot compensate
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: Right MCA stroke → right parietal lobe damage → loss of left-sided spatial awareness → left neglect
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Left hemisphere (dominant) lesions cause APHASIA, not neglect. Right hemisphere lesions cause NEGLECT, not aphasia (in right-handed patients).

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: Right MCA territory ischemic stroke secondary to antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, evidenced by recurrent pregnancy losses, prior DVT, livedo reticularis, and young age without conventional risk factors
supporting_evidence: Young female (28 years) with stroke — mandates unusual etiology search
Recurrent pregnancy loss (2 first-trimester abortions) — APS clinical criterion
Prior DVT — APS clinical criterion (venous thrombosis)
Livedo reticularis — strongly associated with APS (Sneddon syndrome)
Arterial (stroke) + venous (DVT) thrombosis in same patient — highly suggestive of APS
No conventional risk factors (no HTN, DM, smoking, AF)

differentials.points_for: Young female, APS features may be secondary to SLE
differentials.why_not: No malar rash, no joint pain, no serositis, no oral ulcers — but need ANA, dsDNA to rule out. Treatment of APS is the same regardless.
Would not explain DVT or recurrent abortions. But PFO should be excluded with bubble echo as a co-factor.
No neck trauma, no headache, no Horner syndrome. Would not explain DVT or abortions.
More common in East Asian populations. Would not explain systemic features (DVT, abortions, livedo).
Usually presents with multifocal symptoms, headache, encephalopathy. Single territory stroke without systemic inflammation is less typical.

investigations.expected: Anticardiolipin IgG/IgM (medium-high titer), anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I IgG/IgM, lupus anticoagulant. Must be positive on 2 occasions >=12 weeks apart. Triple positivity (all 3) = highest risk.
investigations.why: Confirms APS diagnosis (Sydney laboratory criteria). Test BEFORE starting anticoagulation.

investigations.test: CT brain / MRI brain with DWI
investigations.expected: Right MCA territory infarct. MRI superior for acute ischemia (DWI restriction). Multiple territory involvement suggests embolic source.
investigations.why: Confirms stroke location and type

investigations.expected: Assess circle of Willis, carotid, vertebral arteries. Rule out dissection, moyamoya, stenosis.
investigations.why: Vascular imaging essential in young stroke

investigations.expected: ANA, dsDNA, complement C3/C4 — rule out SLE. ANCA if vasculitis suspected.
investigations.why: Determine if APS is primary or secondary to SLE

investigations.expected: Libman-Sacks vegetations (non-bacterial, in APS/SLE), PFO (bubble study with Valsalva), valvular disease, LA thrombus
investigations.why: Cardiac source workup. TEE superior for PFO and aortic arch atheroma.

investigations.expected: Protein C/S, antithrombin III, Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation. Test BEFORE anticoagulation or >2 weeks after stopping.
investigations.why: Complete prothrombotic workup in young stroke

investigations.expected: Fasting. If elevated → check B12, folate, MTHFR mutation
investigations.why: Hyperhomocysteinemia = premature atherosclerosis and thrombosis. Common in Indian vegetarians.

investigations.expected: Exclude sickle cell disease/trait
investigations.why: Sickle cell causes stroke in young via vaso-occlusion

investigations.expected: Rule out paroxysmal AF (may not be caught on single ECG)
investigations.why: Cardioembolic source even in young patients

medical.rationale: APS with arterial thrombosis (stroke) requires lifelong anticoagulation. Target INR 2-3.
medical.specifics: DOACs are NOT recommended for APS (TRAPS trial 2018: rivaroxaban inferior to warfarin in triple-positive APS — trial stopped early due to excess events in rivaroxaban arm). Warfarin is standard of care for APS.

medical.rationale: Adjunct in APS — reduces thrombotic risk through anti-inflammatory and anti-thrombotic properties
medical.specifics: 200-400mg daily. Especially if SLE coexists. Also used in primary APS as adjunct.
Physiotherapy, occupational therapy. Younger patients generally have better rehabilitation potential.

management.interventional: Acute: thrombolysis (alteplase within 4.5h) and thrombectomy (LVO up to 24h) — same criteria regardless of age
management.surgical: Not typically applicable. PFO closure if paradoxical embolism confirmed.
management.this_patient: Start warfarin with target INR 2-3 lifelong (NOT DOACs per TRAPS trial). Confirm APS with repeat APLA panel at 12 weeks. Add hydroxychloroquine. Autoimmune screen to determine primary vs secondary APS. Obstetric counseling for future pregnancies (aspirin + LMWH throughout pregnancy — warfarin is teratogenic in first trimester). Rehabilitation.
management.follow_up: INR monitoring (therapeutic 2-3). Repeat APLA panel at 12 weeks for confirmation. Monitor for SLE development (serial ANA). Obstetric counseling. Rehabilitation progress. Screen for other organ involvement (renal, cardiac).

complications.mechanism: >=3 organs involved within 1 week with histopathological evidence of small vessel thrombosis. Multi-organ failure.
complications.presentation: Renal failure, cerebral infarcts, cardiac involvement, pulmonary, cutaneous — developing rapidly
complications.management: Triple therapy: anticoagulation + high-dose steroids + PLEX or IVIg. Rituximab + eculizumab in refractory. Mortality ~50%.

complications.mechanism: Subtherapeutic INR or persistent high antibody titers
complications.presentation: New stroke, DVT, PE, or other thrombotic event while on warfarin
complications.management: Ensure INR 2-3 consistently. Some advocate higher target (3-4) for arterial events. Add hydroxychloroquine. Consider rituximab.

complications.presentation: GI bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage, other sites
complications.management: Regular INR monitoring, patient education, avoid interacting drugs/foods

vivas.question: How do you approach the etiological workup of stroke in a young patient?
vivas.answer: Step 1: Confirm stroke (sudden focal deficit, CT/MRI). Step 2: Ischemic vs hemorrhagic (CT). Step 3: Vascular territory (MCA/ACA/PCA). Step 4: Etiological workup — four categories: (A) Cardiac: Echo, ECG, Holter, bubble study (PFO). (B) Vascular: CTA/MRA (dissection, moyamoya, FMD, vasculitis). (C) Hematological: APLA panel, thrombophilia screen, Hb electrophoresis, homocysteine. (D) Other: drug screen, infections (HIV, syphilis, TB meningitis), genetic (CADASIL/Fabry/MELAS), hormonal (OCP, pregnancy). Step 5: Treat based on etiology — this is where young stroke management differs from elderly (not just antiplatelets + statins).

follow_ups.question: What is the most common etiology of young stroke in India?
follow_ups.answer: Cardioembolism (20-35%) — particularly rheumatic heart disease (still common in India, unlike Western countries where PFO and non-valvular AF dominate). Hematological causes (APLA, etc.) account for 5-15%. Cryptogenic (no cause found) in 25-40%.

vivas.question: Why are DOACs not recommended in APS?
vivas.answer: The TRAPS trial (2018) randomized triple-positive APS patients to rivaroxaban vs warfarin. Trial STOPPED EARLY due to excess thromboembolic events (12%) AND major bleeding in rivaroxaban arm vs 0% events in warfarin arm. Why DOACs fail: APS pathophysiology involves multiple coagulation pathways (thrombin generation, complement, platelet activation, endothelial activation). DOACs target only single factors (Xa or thrombin), while warfarin inhibits multiple vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X). Warfarin provides broader anticoagulation coverage needed for the complex prothrombotic state of APS.

follow_ups.question: What is the lupus anticoagulant paradox?
follow_ups.answer: Lupus anticoagulant PROLONGS the aPTT in vitro (hence 'anticoagulant') — it interferes with phospholipid-dependent coagulation assays. But in vivo it is PROthrombotic. The paradox: the test suggests anticoagulation, but the clinical effect is thrombosis. This is because LA antibodies target beta-2 GP1/phospholipid complexes → activating endothelium and platelets in vivo, while in vitro they interfere with the phospholipid surface needed for clotting assays.

vivas.question: What is Sneddon syndrome?
vivas.answer: Sneddon syndrome = livedo reticularis + cerebrovascular events (stroke/TIA). A clinical syndrome, not a specific disease. Most common association: antiphospholipid antibody syndrome (but can occur without aPL antibodies). Pathology: non-inflammatory thrombotic vasculopathy of small/medium arteries in skin and brain. Livedo reticularis + recurrent strokes in a young patient → check APLA panel. Some cases are aPL-negative Sneddon syndrome — mechanism unclear.

vivas.question: What is paradoxical embolism through a PFO?
vivas.answer: A venous thrombus (from deep veins) crosses from right to left heart through a PFO → embolizes to cerebral arteries → ischemic stroke. The 'paradox': venous thrombus causes arterial event. Requires: (1) source of venous thrombus, (2) right-to-left shunt (PFO/ASD), (3) transient rise in right atrial pressure (Valsalva/coughing/straining). PFO present in 25% general population but 40-50% of cryptogenic young strokes. Diagnosed by: TCD bubble study (screening), TTE with agitated saline, TEE (gold standard). Treatment: PFO closure device + antiplatelet in selected patients (CLOSE, RESPECT, DEFENSE-PFO trials).

vivas.question: What is TB meningitis arteritis and how does it cause stroke?
vivas.answer: TB meningitis produces thick inflammatory exudate in the basal cisterns of the brain. This exudate engulfs the arteries running through the basal cisterns (especially MCA and ACA at the circle of Willis) → inflammatory infiltration of vessel walls (endarteritis obliterans) → vessel narrowing/occlusion → ischemic stroke. Most commonly: MCA territory infarcts and basal ganglia lacunar infarcts. Clinical: fever + meningismus + cranial nerve palsies (6th, 3rd — traversing the exudate) + stroke. CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein.
